value_raw: The Algarve region's population swells from roughly 450,000 to more than 1.5 million during peak summer tourism, directly straining the region's public hospitals specifically — a doctor shortage alongside the seasonal demand spike, with tourists filling emergency-room waiting rooms and pushing local residents' own appointments further out during the same period
notes: algarvedailynews.com, portugalresident.com, checked 2026-07-11. A real, dated, structural finding distinct from Lagos's own already-documented town-level seasonal population doubling (Community row above) — it's the region's healthcare-system capacity specifically absorbing that same swing, not just restaurants and housing. Neither Lisbon's nor Porto's own healthcare research shows an equivalent finding

value_raw: Own public hospital (Hospital de Lagos — relocated into a former private facility in 2022; 44 inpatient beds, 2 operating rooms, consultations, lab and imaging; explicitly described as a 'Basic Emergency Service' tier, not full-acuity ED capability, with named specialties in pediatrics, palliative medicine, oncology, and an ophthalmology reference center, the largest in the Algarve). Faro's larger hospital ~40 minutes by car for more complex needs; true specialist/transplant-level care routes to Lisbon specifically (neither Lagos's nor Faro's hospital is a named transplant center)
notes: sulinformacao.pt, myhospitalnow.com, checked 2026-07-11. Supersedes this row's earlier 2026-07-07 version now that the hospital's actual size/service-tier is on file. Genuinely closer, day to day, than the Algarve's "3-hour drive to Lisbon" reputation implies for routine and most emergency care — that longer trip is a real possibility only for the most specialized care

fact_label: Numbeo Health Care Index (Faro proxy — no Lagos-specific page)
unit: index score (10 contributors, last updated 10 Sept 2025)
notes: Numbeo has no Lagos-specific page — same collision/no-coverage pattern this file's cost-of-living Expatistan cross-check already found. Faro (the Algarve's regional capital and hospital hub) is the nearest comparable, at the lowest reading of any Portuguese location checked (Lisbon 72.38, Porto 78.72, national 72.03) — stated explicitly as a Faro proxy, not a Lagos number, graded low confidence given the small sample. numbeo.com/health-care/in/Faro, checked 2026-07-11

fact_label: Named transplant-capable hospitals + major public/private network
value_raw: Hospital Curry Cabral (kidney/liver) and Hospital de Santa Marta (heart/lung) — two of the country's named transplant centers; Hospital de Santa Maria as the major public general hospital; a dense private network commonly used by expats for English-speaking staff and shorter waits: Hospital da Luz (several sites, incl. the former 'British Hospital' location, Torres de Lisboa), Hospital CUF Descobertas (24/7 private emergency), and CMIL (Clínica Médica Internacional de Lisboa, Anglo-Portuguese staff, 24-hour emergency service aimed specifically at expats/visitors)
notes: Expatica, internationalinsurance.com, flyreva.com, CMIL's own site, checked 2026-07-11

Essentially identical to the national 72.03 figure — Lisbon reads as the researched location that most fully represents "the national average" rather than a local outlier in either direction. Sub-scores echo the national public/private split: private-sector scores run 81-88, public-sector wait-time satisfaction is the weak point at 38.57 ("Low") — the lowest public wait-time reading of any of the three researched Portuguese locations. numbeo.com/health-care/in/Lisbon

value_raw: In-city for both public and private emergency care. Public: Centro Hospitalar Universitário de São João (CHUSJ — Porto's primary 24/7 public emergency center, ~420 emergency episodes/day, full medical/surgical specialties) and Centro Hospitalar Universitário de Santo António (CHUdSA — a second full general hospital, itself a named kidney-transplant center). Private: CUF Porto, Lusíadas
notes: Wikipedia, CHUP's own site, travelinti.com, checked 2026-07-11. Supersedes this row's earlier `[GAP — no Porto-specific healthcare-access detail found this pass]` placeholder now that named facilities are on file — closes the gap named in the healthcare-criterion backfill package (Package B), same pattern as Lisbon's own row

Higher than both Lisbon (72.38) and the national figure (72.03) — skill (85.48), equipment (83.41), and cost-satisfaction (80.29) sub-scores all outscore Lisbon's own. Rests on 106 contributors against Lisbon's 239, a real sample-size gap; no Porto-specific public-sector wait-time sub-score was found this pass the way Lisbon's 38.57 ("Low") figure was. numbeo.com/health-care/in/Porto, checked 2026-07-11

fact_label: D7 Visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
value_raw: Yes
notes: Pensions, rental/real-estate income, transferable equity, IP, financial investment all explicitly named as qualifying — the route built for a passive-income shape, not D8.
One times Portugal's own minimum wage, +50% for a spouse, +30% per dependent child — a notably easier income bar than Mexico's Temporary Resident Visa (~$4,300-4,800/month)
Same 50%/30% dependent add-ons as the income threshold above

fact_label: D7/D8/Golden Visa convert to Portuguese permanent residency
value_raw: Yes
notes: All three convert to national PR directly under Article 80 of Law 23/2007 after 5 years' legal temporary residence (counted from the first residence card) — confirmed across 3 independent sources per route separately, not one source generalizing across all three. Portugal needs no EU Long-Term Residence Directive backstop the way Crete's Digital Nomad Visa does. (No single group_key assigned — this row spans all three routes at once rather than naming one, so it doesn't cleanly fit the single-route group convention; see extraction report.)

fact_label: D8 Visa accepts passive income as qualifying proof
value_raw: No
notes: Built for people actively invoicing remote clients, not people living off passive income — most search-result content wrongly defaults to D8 as "the" Portugal remote-worker visa when D7 is the actual fit for a passive-income shape
Four times minimum wage — the highest income bar of Portugal's two mainstream residency routes, and paired with the row below: passive income doesn't qualify for D8 regardless of amount

Driven by backlog at Portugal's immigration and asylum agency (AIMA) — AIMA reports 93%+ of its backlog resolved, but thousands of applicants and outstanding court decisions were still slowing issuance as of May 2026
Same figure as D7's savings requirement, same dependent add-ons

fact_label: Diligence risk — beachfront/dune public maritime domain
value_raw: Yes
notes: Beachfront/dune property may sit on domínio público marítimo, requiring a concession rather than ordinary freehold — a property-specific diligence question, not a nationality restriction.

fact_label: Dog breed restriction — dangerous and potentially dangerous dog law
value_raw: Breed-blind at the border — Portugal's entry regime does not reference breed at all, so importing a listed-breed dog is not blocked — but a real, ongoing local ownership regime applies once the dog is kept in the country, for seven named breeds and their crosses: Cão de Fila Brasileiro, Dogue Argentino, Pit Bull Terrier, Rottweiler, American Staffordshire Terrier, Staffordshire Bull Terrier, and Tosa Inu. Requirements: a special license from the local parish civil office (Junta de Freguesia), obtainable when the dog is 3-6 months old and revalidated annually; a completed training course for detention of a dangerous dog; muzzle and a leash no longer than 1 metre in public; mandatory civil liability insurance; sterilization for non-pedigree dogs of these breeds between 4 and 6 months of age (pedigree-registered dogs exempt); reported fines of €500-3,750 for non-compliance. Sources diverge on owner-age specifics: one names 18+ as the license-holder age, a second distinguishes license-holding (18+) from the lower bar to actually walk the dog in public (16+)
notes: Legal basis cited directly on DGAV's (Direção-Geral de Alimentação e Veterinária, Portugal's competent veterinary authority) own dangerous-animals information page, fetched and read directly: Decreto-Lei n.º 315/2009 (29 October), amended by Lei n.º 46/2013 (4 July). The breed list is set by Portaria n.º 422/2004, cross-corroborated via a municipal government's own published PDF (Câmara Municipal de Santa Maria da Feira) alongside SEO-aggregator convergence — but the Portaria's and Decreto-Lei's own operative statutory text was not independently read this pass (pgdlisboa.pt gave connection errors on two attempts). Insurance basis: Portaria n.º 585/2004. The 18-vs-16 owner-age split was not independently verified against the amended statute either. Applies identically regardless of which Portugal location (Lisbon, Porto, or Lagos) the dog lives in — a genuinely national fact

value_raw: Low-to-mid teens among European health systems (12th, 13th, and 14th all cited across different write-ups, possibly different edition-years)
notes: Not independently verified against the EHCI's own primary report this pass — directionally "solidly mid-to-upper-tier Europe," not confirmed to one precise number

fact_label: Foreigner healthcare access route, by stay length
value_raw: D7/D8/Golden-Visa resident: national health service (SNS) eligibility once registered (real weeks-to-months registration lag; private insurance bridges it). Short-stay visitor/tourist: no SNS eligibility at all — public hospitals treat emergencies but require proof of travel/health insurance or direct payment
notes: Expatica/gov.uk hospital-guide sources, checked 2026-07-11. Stated for both stay lengths per this project's duration-neutral standard — a real, structural access-tier difference, not a footnote, for any reader whose intended stay is shorter than residency

value_raw: 3-6+ months processing; 4-12 weeks (sometimes 3-4 months) card delivery after approval
notes: Two distinct clocks — approval and physical card delivery — both real friction points, not one combined figure

value_raw: Investment fund (€500,000+); job creation; scientific-research donation; cultural-heritage donation
notes: Real estate eliminated as a qualifying route October 2023, all thresholds, urban and low-density areas alike — every remaining route is an investment-scale commitment (€500,000+ or equivalent), documented here for completeness only

fact_label: Lived first-hand account of a completed D7/D8/Golden Visa to PR conversion under Article 80
value_raw: [GAP]
source_ref: [GAP — none found this pass; source file names a likely timing reason, not an effort gap]
notes: D7's current form and D8 both only launched ~2022, so the first real cohorts are only now approaching, or haven't yet reached, the 5-year mark as of mid-2026 — the reason the divergence flag on the conversion row above stays "Not yet checked" rather than resolved

value_raw: No (closed)
notes: The visa now has to be secured through a consulate before arrival — older, pre-2026 blog content still describes this loophole as available, worth naming plainly since it's actively wrong now

value_raw: 10 (7 for EU/CPLP nationals)
notes: Extended from 5 years — a real, material lengthening of the path to citizenship specifically. Does not change the PR-eligibility timeline above, which still tracks the original 5-year Article 80 clock — the two clocks are no longer aligned the way they used to be

Sub-scores split sharply by sector: private care skill/equipment/speed 82-88 ("Very High"); public-sector wait-time satisfaction just 41.57 ("Low") against 79.19 public cost-satisfaction. numbeo.com/health-care/country_result.jsp?country=Portugal, checked 2026-07-11

value_raw: Same core EU/DGAV entry framework as dogs (microchip before rabies vaccination, 15-week age floor, titer test waived for listed-country origins including the US, no routine quarantine if compliant, 48-hour PEV advance-notice requirement) — no separate breed-restriction law equivalent to dogs' dangerous-dog statute. One real, lower-confidence divergence found: a single aggregator source states non-5th-generation wolf hybrids, and Savannah and Bengal cats, cannot be imported under Portugal's rules — reads as a wildlife-hybrid-ancestry restriction (CITES-adjacent) rather than the dangerous-animal statute, but was found in exactly one source and not confirmed against DGAV directly
notes: General EU/DGAV framework (Direção-Geral de Alimentação e Veterinária, Portugal's competent veterinary authority) is primary-institutional — same DGAV traveller datasheet as the dog row below; the wolf-hybrid/Savannah/Bengal restriction specifically rests on one aggregator source (pettravel.com) only, graded lower confidence deliberately rather than blended into the primary-sourced framework above it. Flagged in red-flags.md as worth independent verification before a cat owner relies on it

fact_label: Pet import cost — entry-point fee and EU health certificate
value_raw: Entry-point veterinary inspection fee (non-EU-origin pets, paid on-site at the airport): €42.25 for one pet, €84.50 for two to five pets. EU health certificate cost: €80-300 if obtained from a Portuguese/EU veterinarian, or roughly $510-560 if obtained from a US veterinarian before departure (covers the exam, certificate preparation, and the US Department of Agriculture's own export-endorsement service, USDA-APHIS)
notes: pawsabroad.co, petabroad.eu, 2026 — aggregator-sourced, not confirmed against DGAV's own fee schedule directly. Applies nationally at any of the three designated entry-point airports (Lisbon, Porto, Faro)

fact_label: Pet import (dog) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: No rabies antibody titer test required for a dog arriving from a country on the EU's titer-exempt list (the US sits on this tier); a titer test is required only from an unlisted-country origin, drawn 30+ days after first vaccination, and the dog cannot travel until 3 months after the sample-collection date. No routine quarantine for a compliant animal. Portugal's own age floor: entry authorised only for dogs over 15 weeks old (12-week minimum rabies-vaccination age + 21-day validity wait), ISO-compliant microchip implanted before or on the vaccination date, and a 10-day-validity EU health certificate. Mandatory 48-hour advance written notice to the specific Traveller's Point of Entry (Ponto de Entrada de Viajantes, PEV) — Lisbon, Porto, and Faro are the three designated PEV airports. Stated consequence of failed compliance: return to origin or, as a last resort, euthanasia under EU legislation
notes: Portugal's competent veterinary authority (DGAV, Direção-Geral de Alimentação e Veterinária) own traveller datasheet (PDF, reviewed Sept 2025), read directly — dgav.pt/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/dog_cats_ferrets.pdf. As of 22 April 2026, the governing EU instrument is Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2026/131 (20 January 2026) + Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2026/705 (20 March 2026), superseding Regulation (EU) No 576/2013/577/2013 — confirmed 2026-07-14 directly against DGAV's own "Na Europa" page (dgav.pt/vaiviajar/conteudo/conteudo-animais-de-companhia/na-europa/), which cites both instruments by number; closes the gap this row previously left open (a fetch result naming these same instrument numbers had been flagged "suspicious" and deliberately not carried forward on 2026-07-14's first pass, then independently confirmed real by a second verification pass the same day, and now confirmed a third time directly against Portugal's own competent authority rather than EUR-Lex alone). Applies identically regardless of which Portugal location (Lisbon, Porto, or Lagos) is the destination — a genuinely national fact, not location-specific

fact_label: Pet import — governing EU regulatory instrument (2026 update), Portugal-specific confirmation
value_raw: As of 22 April 2026, non-commercial pet movement into/within the EU, including Portugal, runs on Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2026/131 and Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2026/705, superseding the 2013-era regulation numbers (576/2013 / 577/2013) most existing pet-relocation literature still cites. Confirmed directly against DGAV's (Direção-Geral de Alimentação e Veterinária, Portugal's competent veterinary authority) own "Na Europa" page, which names both instruments by number. Of Crete's two named 2026 tightenings: (1) the EU pet passport now restricted to owners' EU-member-state habitual residence — CONFIRMED verbatim in DGAV's own text ("emitido no país europeu onde o proprietário do animal reside habitualmente" — issued in the European country where the owner habitually resides); (2) mandatory microchip scan-and-verify at every entry point, replacing spot checks — confirmed as to legal mechanism and Portugal-specific institutional practice (DGAV veterinarians at the Pontos de Entrada de Viajantes, in collaboration with Alfândega/Customs), but DGAV's own text does not use the exact "every entry, replacing spot checks" phrasing found in Crete's source, a narrow residual gap rather than a live doubt about coverage
notes: dgav.pt/vaiviajar/conteudo/conteudo-animais-de-companhia/na-europa/, fetched directly 2026-07-14. Closes an open follow-up item — the successor-regulation citation and the Portugal-specific applicability check for Crete's two confirmed tightenings. Mirrors CR:pet-import-general--regulatory-instrument-2026-update's shape and finding, independently re-derived against Portugal's own primary source rather than assumed by EU-wide analogy. One honest residual: DGAV's own core traveller datasheet (reviewed Sept 2025, the load-bearing source for this file's operational detail) predates these Jan/March 2026 instruments and has not been confirmed to have been refreshed to reflect them — the "Na Europa" page is a different, evidently newer DGAV page, not independently cross-checked against the datasheet line by line this pass

fact_label: Portugal has three real, currently active visa/residency routes (index row)
value_raw: D7 (passive income); D8 (active remote work); Golden Visa (investment-fund/job-creation/donation — real estate route eliminated 2023)
notes: Each route gets its own row-cluster below rather than being collapsed into one row per criterion

fact_label: Private health insurance — Allianz Saúde new-enrollment age ceiling
value_raw: 60 (standard "Total" module)
notes: allianz.pt, informative note 685, checked 2026-07-11. The strictest new-enrollment ceiling of the three insurers checked this pass; a separate "55 Mais" product exists for the older-enrollee segment

fact_label: Private health insurance — Multicare new-enrollment age ceiling
value_raw: No maximum age
notes: Caixa Geral de Depósitos-backed. cgd.pt/English/Individuals/Insurance/Pages/Multicare-Health-Insurances.aspx, checked 2026-07-11. The most permissive of the three insurers checked this pass — no hard ceiling found for initial enrollment or continued coverage

fact_label: Private health insurance — Médis new-enrollment age ceiling
value_raw: 70 (standard Options 1/2/3); separate 'Saúde Vintage' product targets a 55-75 enrollment window
notes: Millennium bcp-backed. millenniumbcp.pt/en/insurance/health/medis, checked 2026-07-11. No maximum retention age once enrolled

fact_label: Public-system (SNS) wait-time strain — the real access weak spot
value_raw: 30.2% of respondents in a post-pandemic national survey reported waiting 3+ months for a medical appointment; public hospitals commonly run 1+ year waits for a first specialist consultation (ophthalmology cited ~2 years); the national health service's telephone-triage line (SNS 24) is projected to face up to 1 million unanswered calls over the 2025-26 winter season
notes: Portuguese and Health post-pandemic study (imin-portugal.com/portugalpathways.io); PMC study, checked 2026-07-11. A capacity-strain trajectory on the access layer specifically, not clinical competency — private-sector wait-time satisfaction scores 72.27 nationally on the same Numbeo scale

value_raw: open-freehold
notes: Decreto-Lei 348/77's historical rustic/rural-land restriction repealed 1986 by Decreto-Lei 197-D/86; Constitution Article 15's equalization principle is a second, independent citation.

value_raw: Kidney, liver, heart, and lung transplantation performed domestically, concentrated at named centers in Lisbon (Hospital Curry Cabral — kidney/liver; Hospital de Santa Marta — heart/lung) and Porto (Centro Hospitalar Universitário de Santo António), coordinated nationally by the Portuguese Institute of Blood and Transplantation
notes: European Liver Transplant Registry, transplantmedicaltourism/medicaltourism.com coverage, checked 2026-07-11. The country's own two largest cities already carry the highest-acuity domestic capability, not just routine/mid-tier care

value_raw: Yes
notes: No nationality-based geographic limitation, mainland or islands.

value_raw: legitimate

notes: Runs at the higher end for a non-resident buyer closing after Sept 1, 2026, given the flat 7.5% non-resident property transfer tax (IMT) (a residency-status, not nationality, differential); no fiscal-representative fee or tax identification number (NIF)-only administrative gate folded in here.
